title: Nobody knows it all
paras: Nobody knows it all. Let me start with that, because there's a quiet myth in professional life that the people who get on are the ones who manage everything by themselves — and it does a great deal of harm. In more than forty years in planning, I've never met a good planner who succeeded alone. The ones who lasted — who kept their judgement sharp and enjoyed the work — were almost always the ones with a mentor to learn from, a network to lean on, and colleagues they could turn to when a case had them stuck. Planning isn't a solitary craft. It's learned, and sustained, in the company of other planners. And one of the most useful things an experienced hand can tell a younger one is simply this: you're not supposed to do this on your own. The people who seem to be are usually hiding how much help they quietly get.

The plain truth is that planning is far too broad for anyone to master every corner of it. In a single week you might touch heritage, ecology, highways, viability, enforcement, a neighbour dispute and a knotty point of policy — and no one, however experienced, is expert in all of them at once. The most senior planner you know still picks up the phone when a case strays into unfamiliar ground. Still asks a colleague, how would you handle this. Still values a second opinion on a finely balanced judgement. That's not a weakness in them — it's precisely why they're trusted. Good planners know the edges of their own knowledge, and they're entirely comfortable going to someone else to fill the gap.

It follows that needing advice isn't a phase you grow out of. The questions change — the trainee asks how to read a policy, the director asks whether to run a difficult appeal — but the asking never stops, and it shouldn't. Some of the worst mistakes I've seen came from planners who felt they ought to know the answer, and pressed on rather than admit they were unsure. A five-minute conversation would have saved them. The only thing standing in the way was a misplaced sense that a real professional doesn't need to ask. A real professional asks all the time. It's one of the ways the work stays sound.

Now, one from the file — the person who took the time. The most important influence on my early career wasn't a course or a qualification. It was a senior planner in my first authority who, for no reason except decency, let me sit in on his difficult cases, talked me through how he reached a view, and never once made me feel foolish for asking. I learned more from those unhurried conversations than from anything I read. Years later I understood the debt properly, when a nervous graduate asked me something obvious — and I remembered how much a patient answer had once meant. The whole profession runs, quietly, on people taking that kind of time for one another.

title: What a mentor really is
paras: So what is a mentor, really? Simply a more experienced planner who takes an interest in how you're getting on, and is willing to share what they've learned. That's all — and it's a great deal. A good mentor isn't a teacher delivering a syllabus, and isn't a manager judging your performance. They're someone slightly further down the road who'll talk things through with you honestly. They'll tell you when you're on the right track, steer you gently when you're not, and let you learn from their mistakes — so you don't have to make all of them yourself. The relationship can be formal, through a structured scheme. Or it can be entirely informal: the senior colleague you find yourself gravitating towards, whose door is always open, and whose advice you come to trust.

It's a mistake to think mentoring is only for trainees working towards qualification — useful though it is there. The value of having someone wiser to talk to doesn't run out when you pass your assessment. If anything, the questions get harder as you go up. A planner setting up on their own, taking on staff for the first time, weighing whether to fight an appeal, or facing an awkward professional dilemma — they benefit just as much from a trusted, experienced voice as any graduate does. The best planners keep a mentor, or several, throughout their working lives, and become mentors themselves without ever quite stopping being one. Both sides gain from it. There are few more satisfying things in a long career than watching someone you helped along become excellent in their own right.

title: The learning that is never in a textbook
paras: Then there's the learning that is never in a textbook. A large part of what makes a good planner isn't written down anywhere. The policy and the law you can learn from books and courses. But the craft — how to read a room at committee, how to break bad news to a client, when a scheme that ticks the boxes still isn't right, how to write a report that carries a difficult recommendation — that passes from person to person, mostly through ordinary conversation. This is informal learning, and it's where the real depth of the profession lives. You absorb it by working alongside experienced people, watching how they handle things, and talking through the cases that don't have a tidy answer.

That's why talking a difficult case through with a colleague is so valuable — quite apart from the specific advice you get. Explaining a knotty problem out loud, to someone who understands the work, often shows you the answer before they've said a word. And a good colleague will ask the question you hadn't thought to ask yourself. The habit of thinking aloud with trusted people is one of the healthiest a planner can have. It's how you test a judgement before you commit to it. How you catch the flaw you were too close to see. And how, over years, you slowly build the instinct that lets you handle the hard cases well. None of that happens in isolation. It happens in the conversation between planners — and the more freely a workplace allows those conversations, the better everyone in it becomes.

title: The people around you
paras: Now, the people around you. Beyond a mentor, every planner benefits from a wider circle to draw on — the professional network, which sounds grander than it is. In practice, it means the handful of people, in other authorities, in other practices, or met through professional life, whom you can ring with a question, compare notes with, or turn to when you need a steer. The agent you deal with across the table on applications. The officer at the neighbouring council. The planner you trained with and kept in touch with. The people you met at a branch event. Over time, these become a quiet resource of enormous value. When you hit something unfamiliar, the chances are someone in that circle has met it before — and a short conversation saves you hours.

Networks like this are worth tending. Not for anything as calculating as advancement, but because a profession is easier, and more enjoyable, when you're part of it rather than facing it alone. The R-T-P-I and its regional and local branches exist partly for exactly this — a way to meet other planners, keep up with the profession, and find people to learn from. And the Young Planners networks do the same for those starting out. Going along to things, staying in touch with people you like and respect, being willing to help others when they come to you — all of it builds a web of support you'll be very glad of on a difficult day. The planner with a good network is never really stuck, because they always know someone to ask. The one who's kept to themselves faces every problem cold.

title: Looking out for each other
paras: Which brings me to looking out for each other. Support runs in every direction — not just downwards, from senior to junior. Experienced planners have an obvious duty to bring on the people coming up behind them: to be generous with their time, patient with obvious questions, and quick to give credit. And doing so is one of the real pleasures of a long career. But senior people need support too, and often get less of it, because everyone assumes they're fine. The head of service carrying the whole department. The sole practitioner with no colleagues at all. The experienced planner having a hard year. These people can be quietly struggling behind a competent front, and a bit of collegial care aimed upwards, not only down, matters more than we usually admit.

Part of looking out for one another is noticing when a colleague doesn't seem themselves. You don't need to be an expert to see that someone has gone quiet, is snapping uncharacteristically, is staying very late and looking worn, or has lost their usual spark. When you notice, the kind thing is also the simple thing — a quiet, private word, unhurried and without fuss. You have seemed a bit weighed down lately — is everything all right? You're not there to diagnose anything, or to fix their life. You're there to let them know they've been seen, and that someone cares — and to be ready to point them towards more formal help if they want it. Very often, just being asked, by someone who plainly means it, is enough to let a person say out loud that they're finding things hard. And that is usually the first step to things getting better. A profession where people look out for each other in this ordinary way is a far better place to work than one where everyone soldiers on in silence.

title: Asking for help is professional
paras: Asking for help is professional. If there's one idea in this article worth pinning up above the desk, it's that one — asking for help is a mark of professionalism, not a confession of weakness. The instinct to hide difficulty, to pretend to a confidence you don't feel, to treat every request for advice as an admission of inadequacy — it's common in planning, and it's entirely wrong. The professionals I most respect are precisely the ones who say, easily and without embarrassment, I'm not sure about this, let me check. Or, this one's getting on top of me — can we talk it through? That is what competence actually sounds like. It's the person who never asks, never checks and never admits a doubt who should worry you. Because sooner or later, they'll get something badly wrong rather than lose face.

The reason this matters beyond the individual is that a whole team takes its cue from whether asking is safe. If the people at the top clearly value honesty about difficulty — if they ask for help themselves, respond well when others do, and never punish an admission of uncertainty — then everyone below them can do the same, and problems get raised while they're still small. If, instead, asking is treated as failure, people hide their struggles until they become crises, and the work suffers along with the people doing it. Encouraging open, ordinary conversation about pressure and workload — making it as normal to say I'm overloaded this week as to say I'm on top of things — is one of the most valuable things a workplace can do. And it costs nothing but a change of attitude. The strongest teams aren't the ones where nobody ever needs help. They're the ones where asking for it is completely unremarkable.

title: A profession worth staying in
paras: All of this points to something bigger — a profession worth staying in. It adds up to something larger than any individual's coping: the culture of the profession itself, and whether it's a place people want to stay. A supportive culture isn't built by grand initiatives. It's built by countless small acts. The experienced planner who makes time for a graduate. The colleague who talks a difficult case through with you. The quiet word with someone who seems weighed down. The manager who asks for help themselves, and so makes it safe for everyone else. Every planner contributes to that culture, or detracts from it, by how they treat the people around them — and over a career, the contribution adds up to a great deal. The kindness you show a nervous newcomer today shapes the kind of colleague they become in twenty years' time.

And it's worth remembering, in a job that can be demanding and occasionally bruising, that planning is also a genuinely rewarding profession, full of good people. You get to shape places, to solve real problems, to make a difference to how communities live — and to do it alongside colleagues who, in my long experience, are overwhelmingly decent and generous when given half a chance. The planners who build long, successful and happy careers are almost never the lone experts of the myth. They're the ones who found good people to learn from, kept good people around them, offered the same generosity back, and understood that a career in planning isn't a solo endeavour but a shared one. Look after the people around you, and let them look after you, and the work becomes not just survivable but something to enjoy for a lifetime. You are not alone in this. And you were never meant to be.

title: In short
paras: In short, then. Here is the heart of it, gathered in one place.

First — no one masters all of planning. The most senior planners still ask, still check, still want a second opinion. Needing advice isn't a phase you grow out of; the questions just get harder. Second — a mentor is an experienced planner who takes an interest. Not a teacher, not a manager — someone slightly further down the road who talks things through honestly. Keep one, or several, throughout your career, not just as a trainee. Third — the best of the craft is never in a textbook. How to read a committee, how to break bad news, how to sense when a compliant scheme is still wrong — it passes person to person, through conversation. Talk your difficult cases through. Fourth — tend your network. A handful of people you can ring with a question is worth more than you can measure. The planner with a good network is never really stuck; the one who kept to themselves faces every problem cold. Fifth — support runs upwards too. Bring on the juniors, yes. But senior people, sole practitioners and heads of service struggle quietly behind a competent front, and everyone assumes they're fine. Sixth — notice, and have the quiet word. You don't need to diagnose anything. You have seemed weighed down lately — are you all right? That lets a person know they've been seen, and being asked is often enough to let them say it out loud. Seventh — asking for help is what competence sounds like. I'm not sure, let me check — that is the professional. Worry about the one who never asks and never doubts; they'll one day get something badly wrong rather than lose face. Eighth — teams take their cue from the top. When leaders ask for help themselves, and never punish an admission of difficulty, problems get raised while they're small. Make I'm overloaded this week as ordinary to say as its opposite. Ninth — use what the profession offers. The R-T-P-I, its branches and the Young Planners networks exist partly to help you find people to learn from. Go along, stay in touch, and help others when they come to you. Tenth — culture is built from small acts. The time you give a newcomer, the case you talk through, the word with a struggling colleague — over a career, it adds up to whether planning is a profession people want to stay in. And finally — you were never meant to do it alone. The planners with long, happy careers found good people to learn from, kept good people around them, and gave the same back. Let others help you, and the work becomes something to enjoy for a lifetime.
